steps.title: Identify the key finding
steps.content: Several moth genera that emit ultrasonic pulses are unpalatable to bats—these moths possess both acoustic and chemical defenses, not just one or the other.

steps.title: Apply the signal to the choices
steps.content:
The hypothesis: ultrasound evolved because it costs less metabolically than producing noxious chemicals. The implicit premise is that these are alternative strategies — moths evolved ultrasound instead of chemical defenses to save energy.

The finding: several genera emit disruptive ultrasound and are also unpalatable (chemically defended). If these moths already maintain both defenses, the cost-saving rationale for evolving ultrasound falls apart — moths with chemical defenses didn't evolve ultrasound to avoid that cost.
  • A — reframes the finding as a prediction about future research, not a logical implication for the hypothesis.
  • B — speculates that ultrasound evolved for purposes other than echolocation disruption — a claim the study doesn't support.
  • C — correctly states the hypothesis likely doesn't account for all instances: moths with both defenses contradict the cost-saving premise. ✓
  • D — says chemical unpalatability and echolocation disruption are not mutually exclusive, but this restates the finding without naming the implication for the hypothesis.
That matches choice C — C is correct.
